fact: Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.
citation: Two hearts pump blood to the gills; the third pumps to the rest of the body. Their blood uses copper-based hemocyanin, which is blue (National Geographic).

fact: A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance'.
Sea otters hold hands while sleeping so they don't drift apart.
A cheetah can go from 0 to 60 mph in about 3 seconds — faster than most sports cars.
Cows have best friends and get stressed when separated from them.
citation: University of Northampton, Krista McLennan research (2011).

fact: A blue whale's tongue can weigh as much as an elephant.
Penguins can't fly through the air, but they 'fly' through water using their wings as flippers.
A group of crows is called a 'murder'.
Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.
Elephants are afraid of bees — farmers in Africa use beehive fences to protect crops.
A snail can carry 10 times its own body weight.
Polar bears have black skin under their white fur.
A koala sleeps about 18-22 hours a day.
citation: Australia Zoo / San Diego Zoo Wildlife Alliance.

fact: Wombat poop is cube-shaped.
citation: Georgia Tech research (2018), Atherton et al.

fact: A giraffe's heart can weigh as much as a bowling ball — about 25 pounds.
Sharks existed before trees did.
citation: Sharks first appeared around 400 million years ago; trees about 350 million years ago (Smithsonian).

fact: A sloth can take a whole month to digest one leaf.
Butterflies taste food with their feet.
A group of pandas is called an 'embarrassment'.
Owls can rotate their heads up to 270 degrees but cannot move their eyes.
Honeybees can recognize human faces.
An axolotl can regrow its arms, legs, tail, and even parts of its heart and brain.
Goldfish have a memory longer than 3 seconds — they can remember things for at least 5 months.
A platypus has venom in its hind legs (males only).
Tigers have striped skin, not just striped fur.
A blue whale's heartbeat can be heard from over 2 miles away underwater.
Geckos can run across ceilings because their toes have millions of tiny hairs that stick to surfaces.
Hippos sweat a red, oily liquid that protects their skin like sunscreen.
A starfish has no brain and no blood.
Dogs sweat through their paws.
A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
citation: NASA — Venus takes 243 Earth days to spin once, but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

fact: If you could drive a car to the Sun at 60 mph, it would take about 177 years.
Saturn would float in water if you had a big enough bathtub — it's less dense than water.
citation: NASA — Saturn's density is 0.687 g/cm³, less than water's 1.0.

fact: The footprints of Apollo astronauts will stay on the Moon for millions of years because there's no wind or water to erase them.
One million Earths could fit inside the Sun.
citation: NASA — the Sun's volume is about 1.3 million times Earth's.

fact: Jupiter has 95 known moons.
A neutron star is so dense that a sugar-cube-sized piece would weigh about a billion tons.
Mars is sometimes called the Red Planet because rusty iron dust covers its surface.
Olympus Mons on Mars is the tallest volcano in the solar system — almost 3 times the height of Mount Everest.
It rains diamonds on Neptune and Uranus.
citation: Lawrence Livermore Lab research, Nature Astronomy 2017.

fact: Light from the Sun takes about 8 minutes to reach Earth.
There are more stars in the universe than grains of sand on all the beaches of Earth.
Astronauts on the International Space Station see 16 sunrises every day because they orbit Earth every 90 minutes.
The Moon is slowly moving away from Earth — about 1.5 inches farther every year.
Mercury has no atmosphere, so its sky is always black, even during the day.
If you screamed in space, no one could hear you — there's no air to carry sound.
A black hole is so heavy that not even light can escape its pull.
Pluto used to be called a planet, but in 2006 scientists reclassified it as a 'dwarf planet'.
Astronauts can grow up to 2 inches taller in space because their spines stretch in zero gravity.
The Milky Way is just one of about 2 trillion galaxies in the observable universe.
Saturn has rings made mostly of ice and rock — some pieces as small as dust, others as big as houses.
Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system — about 870°F at its surface.
citation: NASA — Venus is hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun, because of its thick CO2 atmosphere.

fact: The International Space Station travels about 17,500 mph — fast enough to go around Earth in 90 minutes.
A galaxy called IC 1101 is over 1,000 times wider than our Milky Way.
Astronaut John Glenn was the first American to orbit Earth, in 1962. He later went back to space at age 77.
There may be life on one of Jupiter's moons — Europa has a giant ocean of liquid water under its icy surface.
A 'shooting star' isn't really a star — it's a tiny piece of rock or dust burning up in Earth's atmosphere.
The Sun is about 4.6 billion years old — and will keep shining for about 5 billion more years.
Jupiter has a huge storm called the Great Red Spot that has been raging for at least 350 years.
If you fell into a black hole, you would be stretched like spaghetti by gravity. Scientists call this 'spaghettification'.
Your body has about 60,000 miles of blood vessels — enough to wrap around Earth more than twice.
citation: Cleveland Clinic / National Institutes of Health.

fact: Your stomach gets a new lining every 3-4 days because stomach acid would otherwise dissolve it.
Your brain uses about 20% of your body's energy even though it's only 2% of your body weight.
You are about 1 cm taller in the morning than at night — gravity squishes your spine during the day.
Your sense of smell is about 10,000 times more sensitive than your sense of taste.
Babies are born with about 300 bones, but adults have only 206 — some fuse together as you grow.
Your tongue is the strongest muscle for its size in your body.
You produce about 1-2 liters of saliva every day — that's enough to fill 2 large water bottles.
Your heart beats about 100,000 times each day.
Goosebumps used to make our ancestors look bigger and scarier when they were cold or frightened.
Your fingernails grow about 4 times faster than your toenails.
You shed about 30,000 skin cells every minute — that's most of the dust in your house!
Your eyes can tell the difference between about 10 million different colors.
Your ears never stop growing throughout your entire life.
If you spread out all your lungs' air sacs flat, they would cover about half a tennis court.
You can't tickle yourself because your brain knows it's coming.
Your bones are about 5 times stronger than steel of the same weight.
citation: NIH / National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases.

fact: Sneezes can travel up to 100 miles per hour.
Every person has a unique tongue print, just like fingerprints.
Your body has enough iron in it to make a small nail (about 3 inches long).
When you blush, the lining of your stomach also turns red.
Your nose can remember about 50,000 different smells.
Bones repair themselves when they break — that's how a cast helps the bone heal in the right shape.
Your brain has about 86 billion nerve cells (neurons).
citation: Suzana Herculano-Houzel research, Journal of Comparative Neurology 2009.

fact: Laughing for 15 minutes burns about 40 calories.
Your liver can grow back even if as much as 75% of it is removed.
Hair grows about half an inch each month.
You blink about 15-20 times a minute — that's almost 30,000 times a day.
Your kidneys filter about 50 gallons of blood every single day.
Adults have about 5 liters of blood — that's about 1.3 gallons.
The word 'dinosaur' means 'terrible lizard' in Greek.
citation: Coined by Sir Richard Owen in 1842.

fact: T. rex had teeth as long as a banana.
Some dinosaurs had feathers — and some scientists think modern birds are basically living dinosaurs.
The biggest dinosaur ever found, Argentinosaurus, was as long as 3 school buses lined up.
Stegosaurus had a brain the size of a walnut — even though it was the size of a bus.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History.

fact: Dinosaurs lived on Earth for about 165 million years — humans have been around for only about 300,000 years.
Velociraptors were actually only about the size of a turkey, not the giant lizards in the movies.
Some dinosaur fossils have been found right here in Texas — including a T. rex relative called Tyrannosaurus.
citation: Texas Memorial Museum / Perot Museum of Nature and Science.

fact: Most dinosaurs ate plants, not meat.
Dinosaur poop fossils are called 'coprolites' — and scientists study them to figure out what dinosaurs ate.
The fastest dinosaur could run about 25 mph — about as fast as a person on a bicycle.
Brachiosaurus was so tall it could have eaten leaves off the top of a 4-story building.
Dinosaurs went extinct about 66 million years ago, probably because a huge asteroid hit Earth.
citation: Alvarez et al., Science 1980 / Chicxulub crater research.

fact: Some dinosaurs swallowed rocks called 'gastroliths' to help grind up the plants in their stomachs.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History.

fact: Triceratops had three horns and a giant frill on its head — almost like a built-in shield.
Some dinosaur eggs were as big as a basketball.
citation: Paleontology, sauropod and oviraptor egg fossils.

fact: Spinosaurus is the only known dinosaur that could swim — it had crocodile-like jaws and a paddle tail.
A baby T. rex started out covered in fluffy feathers — like a giant chick.
Ankylosaurus had a club at the end of its tail that could break a T. rex's leg bones.
The smallest dinosaur known was about the size of a chicken.
Some dinosaurs lived in herds, like cattle, while others were lone hunters.
Birds are technically dinosaurs — the only group that survived the extinction.
T. rex couldn't see things that didn't move — that's a myth. T. rex actually had excellent eyesight.
citation: Kent Stevens, University of Oregon research 2006.

fact: Mary Anning, born in 1799, was one of the first famous fossil hunters — and she was a girl who found her first ichthyosaur skeleton when she was just 12.
Some dinosaur tracks in Glen Rose, Texas, are over 100 million years old.
citation: Dinosaur Valley State Park, Texas Parks and Wildlife.

fact: Dinosaurs walked on land that we now call North America when it was part of a huge supercontinent called Pangaea.
Some dinosaurs had crests on their heads that worked like trumpets — they could make loud, low calls.
A dinosaur's lifespan could be as long as 70-80 years for big sauropods.
The asteroid that wiped out the dinosaurs hit near what's now Mexico — the crater is called Chicxulub.
Dinosaur fossils can still be found in many U.S. states — including Texas, Montana, Colorado, and Utah.
Honey never spoils — archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that's still edible.
Bananas grow upside down, not down — they curl up toward the sun.
Carrots used to be purple, not orange. Orange carrots were developed in the 1500s.
Pineapples take about 2 years to grow.
A strawberry is not technically a berry, but a banana is.
citation: USDA Botanical Classification — true berries have seeds inside, not on the outside.

fact: Chocolate was once used as money by the Aztecs — cocoa beans were so valuable.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of the American Indian.

fact: Watermelons are about 92% water.
Peanuts are not nuts — they're legumes, like beans and peas.
There are over 7,500 different types of apples grown around the world.
The world's most expensive pizza cost $12,000 and was topped with edible gold.
citation: Guinness World Records — Renato Viola, Salerno, Italy.

fact: Mac and cheese is so popular in the U.S. that there's a National Mac and Cheese Day on July 14.
Avocados are a fruit, not a vegetable.
Texas is the #1 producer of cantaloupes and grapefruits in the United States.
The hottest pepper in the world, the Carolina Reaper, is over 200 times hotter than a jalapeño.
citation: Guinness World Records / PuckerButt Pepper Company.

fact: Ice cream was first invented in China over 4,000 years ago — made with snow and milk.
Cucumbers are 96% water, even more than watermelon.
Pasta has been around for at least 4,000 years — and probably wasn't brought to Italy from China by Marco Polo as people say.
A single cob of corn has about 800 kernels arranged in 16 rows — always an even number.
Mushrooms are more closely related to humans than to plants.
Texas is the largest producer of cattle in the United States.
Pizza was originally a food for poor people in Naples, Italy.
Cheese is made from milk that's been intentionally spoiled by good bacteria.
Texas grows about 80% of the watermelons in the United States.
citation: Texas Department of Agriculture / National Watermelon Promotion Board.

fact: An ear of corn always has an even number of rows.
Chewing gum has been around for about 9,000 years — ancient people chewed birch bark resin.
Bread used to be one of the most valuable things you could trade.
Lemons float because they have less density than water, but limes sink.
citation: Royal Society of Chemistry — lemons have air pockets in their peels.

fact: Apples float because 25% of their volume is air.
citation: USDA Food Composition Database / Penn State Extension.

fact: Sugar can keep fresh almost forever if you keep it away from moisture.
citation: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service.

fact: It takes about 35-50 gallons of sap to make just one gallon of maple syrup.
citation: U.S. Forest Service / Vermont Maple Sugar Makers Association.

fact: The Great Wall of China is over 13,000 miles long.
citation: China State Administration of Cultural Heritage 2012 survey.

fact: Australia is the only country that is also a continent.
The Pacific Ocean is so big it covers more area than ALL the land on Earth combined.
Russia is so big that when it's morning in one part of Russia, it's nighttime in another.
citation: Russia spans 11 time zones — CIA World Factbook.

fact: Mount Everest, the tallest mountain on Earth, is still growing — about 4mm taller every year.
The deepest place on Earth is the Mariana Trench in the Pacific — about 36,000 feet deep.
Africa is home to the world's longest river — the Nile, at over 4,100 miles.
There are about 195 countries in the world today.
The Amazon Rainforest is so big it produces about 20% of the world's oxygen.
Iceland keeps growing by 2 cm every year because the Atlantic Ocean is splitting it apart.
Lake Baikal in Russia holds about 20% of all the unfrozen freshwater on Earth.
citation: UNESCO World Heritage / Russian Academy of Sciences.

fact: The Sahara Desert is bigger than the entire United States.
Antarctica is the windiest, driest, and coldest continent on Earth.
The Dead Sea is so salty you can float without trying — about 10 times saltier than the ocean.
Texas is bigger than every country in Europe except Russia and Ukraine.
citation: Texas: 268,597 sq mi. Comparison with European Union countries.

fact: The Vatican City is the smallest country in the world — you could walk across it in 15 minutes.
Earth has only one official continent that ends with -a (Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, North America, South America) — but only 5 are sometimes counted.
The Andes are the longest mountain range in the world — they stretch across 7 countries in South America.
The Atacama Desert in Chile is so dry, some weather stations have NEVER recorded rain.
If all the ice in Antarctica melted, sea levels would rise about 200 feet.
citation: NASA Earth Observatory / National Snow and Ice Data Center.

fact: Greenland is owned by Denmark, not Greenland.
The Mississippi River starts in Minnesota and flows all the way to the Gulf of Mexico — over 2,300 miles.
Japan has over 6,800 islands.
The Grand Canyon in Arizona is about 277 miles long and over a mile deep.
The Rio Grande forms part of the border between Texas and Mexico for nearly 1,250 miles.
Earth has thousands of volcanoes — and about 1,500 of them are 'active' (could erupt).
Mount Etna in Italy has been erupting on and off for over 2.5 million years.
citation: INGV (Italian National Institute of Geophysics and Volcanology).

fact: Africa is the only continent that has land in all four hemispheres — northern, southern, eastern, AND western.
The Eiffel Tower in Paris is about 6 inches taller in the summer because the metal expands in heat.
citation: Société d'Exploitation de la Tour Eiffel.

fact: Earth's surface is about 71% water and only 29% land.
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the building of the first pizza shop in modern America than to the building of the Great Pyramid.
citation: The Great Pyramid was built ~2560 BCE; Cleopatra ruled ~50 BCE; first U.S. pizzeria opened 1905.

fact: Vikings discovered North America about 500 years before Columbus.
citation: Smithsonian / L'Anse aux Meadows UNESCO site.

fact: The shortest war in history lasted only 38 minutes — between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896.
Ancient Egyptians invented the 365-day calendar around 4,500 years ago.
The Statue of Liberty was a gift from France to the United States.
citation: National Park Service / Statue of Liberty National Monument.

fact: Ancient Romans had a sport like soccer over 2,000 years ago — they called it 'harpastum'.
Knights' armor weighed about 50 pounds — like wearing a 50-pound backpack while running and fighting.
The first known map in the world was made in Babylon about 2,600 years ago.
Pirates wore eye patches not because they lost an eye, but to keep one eye adjusted to the dark for going below deck.
George Washington had wooden teeth — actually a myth. His false teeth were made of ivory, gold, lead, and human teeth.
The first U.S. flag had 13 stars and 13 stripes — one for each colony.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of American History.

fact: Albert Einstein could not speak fluently until he was about 9 years old.
Ancient Egyptians used to mummify cats — and millions of mummified cats have been found.
Sliced bread was first sold in 1928 — that's why we say 'the best thing since sliced bread'.
citation: Otto Frederick Rohwedder patented the bread-slicing machine, sold in Chillicothe, Missouri.

fact: The Berlin Wall, which divided Germany for almost 30 years, fell in 1989.
Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt — the word 'salary' comes from the Latin word for salt.
Christopher Columbus made four trips across the Atlantic and never realized he had reached a continent unknown to Europeans.
Anne Frank's diary, written during World War II when she was hiding, has been translated into more than 70 languages.
The Wright Brothers' first flight in 1903 lasted only 12 seconds.
citation: Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum.

fact: Rosa Parks was an African American woman who refused to give up her bus seat in 1955 — helping start the Civil Rights Movement.
citation: Library of Congress / National Museum of African American History and Culture.

fact: The first U.S. president, George Washington, never lived in the White House — it wasn't finished until after he died.
Cleopatra was not Egyptian — she was Greek, descended from Ptolemy, one of Alexander the Great's generals.
Texas has its own pledge of allegiance — kids in Texas schools say it after the U.S. Pledge.
Alexander the Great conquered most of the known world by the time he was 30 — and died at 32.
Helen Keller was both deaf and blind from age 19 months — and yet she earned a college degree and wrote 12 books.
The Olympics began in ancient Greece almost 3,000 years ago.
citation: International Olympic Committee — first recorded games in 776 BCE.

fact: Mahatma Gandhi led India to independence using only peaceful protests — no violence.
The Titanic sank in 1912 after hitting an iceberg on its very first voyage.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of American History.

fact: The Pony Express, which delivered mail across the U.S. by horseback, only existed for about 18 months before the telegraph replaced it.
citation: Pony Express National Museum, St. Joseph, Missouri.

fact: Harriet Tubman escaped slavery and then helped about 70 enslaved people escape to freedom through the Underground Railroad.
citation: National Park Service / Harriet Tubman Underground Railroad National Historical Park.

fact: The microwave oven was invented by accident in 1945 when a chocolate bar melted in an engineer's pocket.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of American History — Percy Spencer at Raytheon.

fact: Velcro was inspired by burrs that stuck to a Swiss engineer's dog after a walk.
Thomas Edison didn't invent the lightbulb — he just made it last longer. Earlier inventors had bulbs that burned out in seconds.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of American History.

fact: Bubble wrap was originally invented as wallpaper — but no one wanted to buy it.
citation: Smithsonian Magazine — Sealed Air Corporation history.

fact: Play-Doh was originally sold as wallpaper cleaner before someone realized kids loved playing with it.
Post-it Notes were invented when a scientist trying to make super-strong glue accidentally made super-WEAK glue instead.
The first computer mouse was made out of wood in 1964.
citation: Douglas Engelbart, Stanford Research Institute / Computer History Museum.

fact: Alexander Graham Bell, the inventor of the telephone, called his first phone call to his assistant.
citation: Bell said "Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you." Library of Congress.

fact: Chocolate chip cookies were invented by accident in 1938 when a baker ran out of melted chocolate and used chunks instead.
citation: Ruth Wakefield, Toll House Inn, Massachusetts.

fact: Penicillin, the first antibiotic, was discovered by accident in 1928 when mold grew on a forgotten dish in a lab.
The first email was sent in 1971 — and the inventor forgot what it said.
The Slinky was invented when an engineer accidentally knocked a spring off a table and saw it walk down a stack of books.
citation: Richard James, 1943. National Toy Hall of Fame.

fact: Bicycles were invented in 1817 — before the bicycle was just walking, riding a horse, or going by carriage.
Crayola crayons come in over 200 different colors today, including ones named 'macaroni and cheese' and 'screaming green'.
The first photograph ever taken needed 8 hours of exposure to capture an image.
Coca-Cola was originally sold as a medicine for headaches.
Toilet paper wasn't invented in the United States until the 1850s.
George Washington Carver invented over 300 uses for the peanut.
Sliced bread was invented in 1928 in Missouri — and was banned briefly during WWII to save metal.
The roller coaster was invented over 200 years ago in Russia — they were giant ice slides used in winter.
The frisbee was named after a pie tin company — kids used to throw the empty tins for fun.
citation: Smithsonian — Frisbie Pie Company, Bridgeport, Connecticut.

fact: Computer 'mouse' got its name because the cord coming out the back looked like a tail.
Margaret Knight invented a machine that makes flat-bottomed paper bags. She had to fight in court to prove the invention was hers, and won.
citation: U.S. National Inventors Hall of Fame.

fact: Garrett Morgan invented the three-light traffic signal in 1923 — adding the yellow 'caution' light to keep drivers safer.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History.

fact: The first computer programmer was a woman — Ada Lovelace, in 1843, almost 100 years before computers existed.
The Eraser on top of a pencil was invented in 1858 by an art student.
Stephanie Kwolek invented Kevlar — the super-strong material in bulletproof vests.
citation: DuPont / National Inventors Hall of Fame.

fact: Levi Strauss invented blue jeans during the California Gold Rush, originally as tough work pants for miners.
Texas Instruments, a Texas company, invented the handheld calculator in 1967.
The first vending machine was invented in ancient Greece almost 2,000 years ago — it dispensed holy water.
The number zero was invented in India about 1,500 years ago.
There is a number called googol — it's the number 1 followed by 100 zeros.
citation: Edward Kasner coined the term in 1920; the Google company name is a misspelling.

fact: If you add up all the numbers from 1 to 100, you get 5,050.
citation: Mathematical sum: n(n+1)/2 = 100*101/2 = 5,050. Famously solved by 8-year-old Carl Gauss.

fact: Multiply any number by 9 and the digits of the answer add up to 9. Example: 9 × 7 = 63, and 6 + 3 = 9.
citation: Property of multiples of 9 — well-known elementary math pattern.

fact: The word 'mathematics' comes from a Greek word meaning 'that which is learned'.
Pi (π) is the number you get when you divide a circle's distance around by its distance across — it goes on forever without repeating.
citation: Pi has been calculated to over 100 trillion digits.

fact: A jiffy is an actual unit of time — about 1/100th of a second.
citation: Originally a chemistry term coined by Gilbert Newton Lewis.

fact: If you fold a piece of paper 42 times, it would be thick enough to reach the Moon (in theory).
citation: Mathematical thought experiment — each fold doubles the thickness; 0.1mm × 2^42 ≈ 400,000 km.

fact: Most clocks have Roman numerals showing IIII instead of IV for the number 4.
citation: Horological tradition; balances the visual weight with VIII on the opposite side.

fact: A 'baker's dozen' is 13 — bakers added an extra loaf to avoid being punished for shorting customers.
citation: Oxford English Dictionary — medieval English bakery tradition.

fact: There are about 8 billion people in the world today.
citation: United Nations Population Division — 8 billion reached in November 2022.

fact: A million seconds is about 11.5 days. A billion seconds is about 31.7 years.
Even numbers always end in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8. Odd numbers end in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9.
The number 4 is unique — it's the only number that has the same number of letters as its value.
The Aztecs used a base-20 number system. We use base-10 because we have 10 fingers.
A 'fortnight' means 14 days (two weeks). The word comes from 'fourteen nights'.
If you have 23 random people in a room, there's about a 50% chance two of them share a birthday. It's called the Birthday Paradox.
The plus sign (+) and minus sign (−) were first used in Europe in the 1500s.
citation: History of mathematical notation — Johannes Widmann, 1489.

fact: Cleopatra studied advanced mathematics — she was very well educated for her time.
Ancient Egyptians used fractions over 4,000 years ago, but they only used unit fractions (like 1/2, 1/3, 1/4).
If you wrote out a googol, you would need a piece of paper bigger than the visible universe to fit all the zeros at human reading size.
The equals sign (=) was invented in 1557 by a Welsh mathematician.
citation: Robert Recorde, "The Whetstone of Witte." Smithsonian.

fact: The word 'hundred' comes from the Old Norse word 'hundrath' — and it originally meant 120, not 100.
The largest prime number found so far has over 24 million digits.
citation: Great Internet Mersenne Prime Search (GIMPS) — discovered 2018.

fact: Three is the smallest 'odd prime' number. (Why isn't 2 odd? Because 2 is even.)
Negative numbers were invented in China about 2,200 years ago.
citation: Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art, Han Dynasty.

fact: There are exactly 525,600 minutes in a regular year — and yes, that's the number from the famous song.
citation: 365 days × 24 hours × 60 minutes = 525,600. Lyrics from 'Rent' (Jonathan Larson).

fact: Round numbers like 100 are sometimes called 'powers of 10' — 100 is 10×10, 1,000 is 10×10×10, and so on.
Multiplying by 11 has a trick: 11 × 23 = 2[2+3]3 = 253. (Add the two digits, put the answer in the middle!)
citation: Standard mental math trick — works for any two-digit number where the digit sum is single-digit.

fact: A 'score' is 20 — Abraham Lincoln's 'Four score and seven years ago' means 4×20+7 = 87 years.
citation: Lincoln, Gettysburg Address, 1863. Oxford English Dictionary.

fact: Mozart wrote his first piece of music when he was 5 years old.
A piano has 88 keys — 52 white and 36 black.
The first song ever recorded was 'Au Clair de la Lune' in 1860 — 17 years before Edison's phonograph.
The longest concert ever lasted over 639 years — and it's still going. It's an organ piece in Germany that changes notes once every few years.
citation: John Cage's ASLSP at Halberstadt, Germany — scheduled to end 2640.

fact: The ukulele was invented in Hawaii in the 1880s — its name means 'jumping flea'.
The Beatles wrote over 200 songs together, and many of them are still some of the most popular songs ever.
citation: Apple Corps / Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.

fact: Saxophones are technically woodwind instruments, even though they're made of brass.
citation: They use a wooden reed — that's what makes them woodwinds. Smithsonian Music.

fact: A drum kit can have 4, 5, or more drums plus cymbals. Most rock drummers start with a 5-piece kit.
Some songs are designed to get stuck in your head — they're called 'earworms'.
citation: James Kellaris, University of Cincinnati research.

fact: Texas is home to the world's largest country-and-western dance hall — Billy Bob's Texas in Fort Worth.
Music can actually help plants grow — they grow better with classical music than rock.
The harp is one of the oldest musical instruments — they were played in Egypt over 4,500 years ago.
Most pop songs are between 3 and 4 minutes long because that's how long old radio singles could be.
citation: Recording industry history — 45 rpm single time limits.

fact: A symphony orchestra usually has about 80-100 musicians playing together.
Singing in a group releases the same brain chemical as eating chocolate — it makes you happy.
The world's largest playable guitar is over 43 feet long.
citation: Guinness World Records — built by Indiana high school students, 2000.

fact: Texas musician Willie Nelson is one of the most famous country singers of all time.
The piano was invented around 1700 — before that, people played the harpsichord, which only had one volume (loud).
citation: Bartolomeo Cristofori, Florence. Metropolitan Museum of Art.

fact: Beethoven kept composing music even after he went completely deaf.
Birds learn to sing in much the same way babies learn to talk — by listening to other birds.
citation: Cornell Lab of Ornithology / Nature research.

fact: The shortest pop song ever to top a chart was only 1 minute 40 seconds long.
Trumpets are very old — versions of them have been played for over 3,500 years.
citation: Trumpets found in King Tutankhamun's tomb. British Museum.

fact: The 'Star-Spangled Banner' (the U.S. national anthem) was originally a British drinking song.
citation: Library of Congress — 'To Anacreon in Heaven' melody.

fact: Selena Quintanilla was a famous Tejano singer from Texas — kids in Texas still listen to her music today.
citation: Texas Music Hall of Fame / Smithsonian.

fact: The very first song an astronaut sang in space was 'Jingle Bells' — in 1965.
Most kids' songs use only 5 or 6 different notes — that's why they're easy to sing.
Beyoncé is from Houston, Texas, and is one of the most awarded singers in history.
Some songs are over 5,000 years old — like 'Hurrian Hymn No. 6' found on clay tablets.
citation: Oldest known song notation, ancient Syria. British Museum.

fact: There are about 250 strings inside a regular piano.
The world's loudest sound recorded from a band was Deep Purple in 1972 — three audience members fainted.
In Greek myths, the goddess Athena was born fully grown — popping right out of Zeus's head.
Norse stories say the rainbow is a bridge connecting Earth to a place where the gods lived.
Pegasus, the winged horse, was born from the blood of Medusa after Perseus cut off her head.
In ancient Egypt, people believed cats were sacred — and the goddess Bastet had a cat's head.
King Midas was a Greek king who supposedly turned everything he touched into gold — even his food.
Thor's hammer in Norse stories was named Mjölnir and could only be lifted by Thor himself.
Hercules (Heracles in Greek) had to do 12 nearly impossible tasks — like cleaning huge stables in a single day.
citation: Greek mythology — Twelve Labors of Heracles.

fact: Dragons appear in stories from China, Europe, the Americas, and almost every culture — though they look very different in each.
The unicorn was once thought to be a real animal, even mentioned in old natural history books.
citation: Medieval bestiaries / Pliny the Elder's Natural History.

fact: Egyptian mythology has over 2,000 gods — far more than Greek mythology.
Odin, the Norse god, gave up one of his eyes in exchange for wisdom.
Many cultures have a flood story — like Noah's Ark — but the oldest written one is from ancient Mesopotamia.
Anansi the spider is a famous trickster character in stories from West Africa.
The Greek god Hermes was the messenger of the gods and had winged sandals.
Atlas in Greek myth held the sky on his shoulders — not the Earth, like some people think.
The phoenix is a magical bird that supposedly bursts into flames when it dies — and then is reborn from the ashes.
Pandora's Box in Greek myth was actually a jar — the word got mistranslated 500 years ago.
citation: Hesiod's Works and Days; Erasmus mistranslation, 16th century.

fact: The story of Sleeping Beauty has been told for over 600 years — the oldest written version is from 1330.
Apollo was the Greek god of music, the Sun, and healing — all at once.
In Hindu myths, the elephant-headed god Ganesha is famous for being the remover of obstacles.
Centaurs in Greek myths were creatures with the upper body of a man and the body of a horse.
Loki was the trickster god in Norse mythology — and Thor's adopted brother.
Anubis, the Egyptian god of mummification, had the head of a jackal.
Native Americans have many stories about Coyote, who is often a clever trickster character.
citation: Smithsonian National Museum of the American Indian.

fact: Zeus, the king of Greek gods, threw thunderbolts when he was angry.
Chinese dragons are usually friendly and bring good luck — unlike European dragons that were often dangerous.
In the Polynesian myths, the demigod Maui pulled islands out of the ocean with a giant fishhook.
Mermaids appear in stories from almost every culture in the world that lives near water.
King Arthur and his Knights of the Round Table is a famous English legend — but most historians think there was never a real King Arthur exactly like in the stories.
citation: British historical research / Geoffrey of Monmouth.

fact: Ancient Greeks thought Mount Olympus, a real mountain in Greece, was where their gods lived.
The first robot vacuum cleaner was sold in 2002.
There are robots on Mars right now — they take photos and study rocks.
The first computer ever made weighed 30 tons and filled an entire room.
Today's smartphones have more computing power than the computers NASA used to send astronauts to the Moon.
Robots can be smaller than a grain of rice — scientists are building tiny robots that can swim through your bloodstream.
The first Wi-Fi was invented in Australia in 1992.
Self-driving cars use over 1,400 different sensors to 'see' the road.
Robots help build almost every car you see on the road today.
The internet was invented by scientists who wanted to share research between universities.
There are about 5 billion smartphone users worldwide — more people than have access to clean drinking water.
citation: Pew Research / WHO Joint Monitoring Programme.

fact: QR codes were invented in Japan in 1994 — originally to track car parts.
The first text message was sent in 1992 — it just said 'Merry Christmas'.
A robot solved a Rubik's Cube in 0.38 seconds — way faster than any human.
Drones (flying robots) are used to deliver pizza, medicine, and packages in some places.
Robot dogs are real — some can run, jump, and even open doors.
The first programmer in history was a woman named Ada Lovelace, in 1843.
Some robots can perform surgery more precisely than human doctors — but doctors still control them.
citation: Intuitive Surgical / da Vinci Surgical System.

fact: There are humanoid robots that can do backflips and parkour.
Bluetooth is named after a Viking king named Harald 'Bluetooth' Gormsson, who united Denmark.
AI (artificial intelligence) can now write stories, make pictures, and even compose music.
The longest-lasting robot in space, Voyager 1, was launched in 1977 and is still sending data home from beyond our solar system.
The first webpage was created in 1991 — and it's still online today.
Robots can be used to explore the deepest parts of the ocean where it's too dark and cold for humans.
A YouTube video is uploaded every second of every day — more than 500 hours of video per minute.
The first video game was created in 1958 — it was a simple tennis game shown on an oscilloscope.
Most computer keyboards have a 'QWERTY' layout that was designed in the 1870s to slow typists down — to prevent old typewriters from jamming.
There's a robot that can solve a Lego puzzle in under 4 seconds.
Robots have explored places humans can't go — like inside volcanoes and on other planets.
Some farms use robot tractors that plant and harvest crops by themselves.
The internet uses about 1% of the world's electricity.
The Olympic flame is lit in Greece months before each Olympics and travels by torch all the way to the host city.
A basketball hoop is exactly 10 feet off the ground — the same height as when basketball was invented in 1891.
citation: Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame.

fact: Soccer is the most popular sport in the world — over 4 billion fans watch it.
A golf ball has between 300 and 500 tiny dimples on it — they help it fly farther.
The fastest pitch in baseball history was over 105 mph — by Aroldis Chapman.
A tennis ball is fuzzy because the fuzz helps slow the ball down for better play.
An Olympic swimming pool is 50 meters long — longer than a basketball court.
Texas's biggest high school football stadium can hold over 18,000 fans.
citation: Allen Eagle Stadium, Allen ISD, Texas.

fact: Football helmets first appeared in the 1890s — and they were made of leather, not plastic.
An NBA basketball is bigger than a WNBA basketball and a youth basketball.
The first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens, Greece, in 1896 — and had only 14 countries.
Cycling is the only Olympic sport where someone has won gold in 4 different events at the same Olympics.
A regulation soccer field is bigger than an American football field.
The longest baseball game ever lasted 8 hours and 25 minutes.
citation: Major League Baseball — Brewers vs. White Sox, 1984.

fact: Surfing began in Hawaii hundreds of years ago — and it was originally only allowed for royalty.
Texas is home to the Dallas Cowboys, one of the most valuable sports teams in the world.
Skateboarding became an Olympic sport for the first time in 2020.
The longest field goal ever kicked in NFL history was 66 yards.
Simone Biles is one of the most decorated gymnasts in history — she has 7 Olympic medals.
citation: USA Gymnastics; Biles is from Spring, Texas.

fact: Hockey pucks are frozen before games to keep them from bouncing too much.
Baseball pitchers can grip a baseball over 12 different ways to throw different pitches.
Tennis was originally played indoors in France — and called 'jeu de paume' (game of the palm) because players hit the ball with their hands.
The highest-scoring basketball game ever had 370 total points scored.
The first ever Olympic gold medal in soccer was won by Great Britain in 1908.
Bowling started in ancient Egypt — bowling balls and pins have been found in tombs over 5,000 years old.
The biggest college football stadium is in Michigan — it can hold over 107,000 fans.
Texas hosts more rodeos than any other state.
Olympic athletes have to be at least 16 years old for most sports.
Babe Ruth was probably the most famous baseball player ever — and he hit 714 home runs in his career.
Snowboarding was invented in 1965 by a guy who tied two skis together so his daughter could 'surf' on snow.
Texas has six different state symbols for foods — including chili, barbecue, and pecans.
citation: Texas State Library and Archives Commission.

fact: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is the largest rodeo in the world.
citation: Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo official records.

fact: Texas has more than 3,700 named streams and 15 major river basins.
The Texas state insect is the monarch butterfly.
Texas's state mammal is the longhorn cattle — and the small mammal is the armadillo.
Big Bend National Park in Texas is so dark at night you can see the Milky Way clearly without a telescope.
citation: International Dark-Sky Association — Big Bend has Dark Sky Park status.

fact: The Alamo gets over 1.5 million visitors every year.
Texas grows more cotton than any other state.
The largest bat colony in the world lives under the Congress Avenue Bridge in Austin, Texas — about 1.5 million bats.
Texas's name comes from the Caddo Indian word 'tejas' which means 'friend' or 'ally'.
Texas has more coastline along the Gulf of Mexico than any other state except Florida — about 367 miles.
Padre Island is the longest barrier island in the world — about 113 miles long.
Whataburger started in Corpus Christi, Texas, in 1950 — and is still one of Texas's favorite fast-food chains.
Texas is the only U.S. state that was once an independent country (1836-1845).
Texas has more wind power than any other U.S. state.
More than 200 different languages are spoken in Texas homes.
citation: U.S. Census Bureau / American Community Survey.

fact: Texas's state nickname is the 'Lone Star State' because of the single star on its flag.
citation: Texas State Library and Archives Commission.

fact: The largest Texas city, Houston, is the 4th-largest city in the United States.
Texas borders 4 U.S. states (New Mexico, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Louisiana) and one country (Mexico).
The Caverns of Sonora in Texas have crystal formations so delicate that they've been called the most beautiful cave in the world.
Texas is the only U.S. state with the right to display its flag at the same height as the U.S. flag — because Texas was once its own country.
citation: Common Texas claim — actually, the U.S. Flag Code allows ALL state flags at equal height; Texas is widely-cited for this tradition.

fact: Texas's official state plant is the prickly pear cactus.
Texas has 254 counties — more than any other U.S. state.
Bluebonnets bloom across Texas every spring — they're the official state flower.
citation: Texas State Library and Archives Commission.

fact: The Caddo Lake on the Texas-Louisiana border is the only naturally formed lake in Texas.
Texas's official state dish is chili — and many Texans have strong opinions about how to make it.
Cattle drives along the Chisholm Trail moved millions of Texas longhorns north to Kansas in the late 1800s.
Texas is so big that the western tip is closer to California than it is to the eastern Texas border.
citation: Geographic measurement — El Paso to LA: ~700 mi; El Paso to Texarkana: ~800 mi.

fact: The Texas state capitol building in Austin is taller than the U.S. Capitol building in Washington, D.C.
Texas's state song is called 'Texas, Our Texas' — written in 1924.
citation: Texas State Library and Archives Commission.

fact: A jiffy is a real unit of time — it's about 1/100th of a second.
The word 'set' has the most definitions in the English language — over 430.
There's a country named 'Djibouti' — and yes, kids everywhere giggle about how it sounds.
It is illegal to own only one guinea pig in Switzerland — they get lonely.
Bananas are slightly radioactive — but you'd need to eat 10 million of them at once to get sick.
citation: Bananas contain potassium-40. EPA / Health Physics Society.

fact: There's a town in Norway where the Sun doesn't rise for 2 months each winter — and doesn't set for 2 months in summer.
citation: Tromsø, Norway. Visit Norway / NASA solar geometry.

fact: Sloths can hold their breath underwater for 40 minutes — longer than dolphins.
There's a museum in Iceland dedicated entirely to penises (the penis museum).
citation: Icelandic Phallological Museum, Reykjavík — well-known curiosity.

fact: The longest word in English has 189,819 letters — it's the chemical name for a protein called titin.
citation: Generally not considered a real word; usually omitted from dictionaries.

fact: If you sneezed in space, the sneeze could push you backwards.
There's a town in Texas called Cut and Shoot.
A 'flummery' is a real word — it means nonsense or empty compliments.
If you yelled non-stop for 8 years, 7 months, and 6 days, you'd produce enough sound energy to heat one cup of coffee.
An ant can survive being microwaved because it's so small that the microwaves miss it.
The smell of fresh-cut grass is actually a chemical the grass releases to warn other plants of danger.
There's a real disorder called 'Alice in Wonderland Syndrome' where people think things look bigger or smaller than they are.
A group of jellyfish is called a 'smack'.
The shortest war in history was fought between the United Kingdom and Zanzibar in 1896 — it lasted 38 minutes.
There's a real species of pink fairy armadillo — it's about the size of a chipmunk and lives in Argentina.
The dot over a lowercase 'i' or 'j' is called a 'tittle'.
Wombats poop cube-shaped poop.
citation: Georgia Tech research, Atherton et al. 2018.

fact: If you tried to count to one billion out loud, it would take you about 31 years.
A group of unicorns is called a 'blessing'.
Birds don't urinate (pee) — their poop and pee come out together as the white-and-dark splat.
It's impossible to lick your own elbow. (Try it!)
citation: Human anatomy — the arm is too short.

fact: There's a town in Texas called 'Telephone' — population about 200.
There's a real medical condition called 'exploding head syndrome' — people hear loud noises that aren't there as they fall asleep.
Your shadow has its own scientific study — it's called 'sciagraphy'.
The Eiffel Tower can be 6 inches taller in summer than in winter, because the metal expands when it's hot.
citation: Société d'Exploitation de la Tour Eiffel.

fact: There are about 1.4 BILLION insects for every single human on Earth.
